tags:
  - workshop

---
# What Is Facilitation?
## A Workshop on Facilitation of Workshops
##### Dan Ryan January 2024
## Basic Idea

Perhaps take facilitator guide from workshop we all attended and work through exercises by exercise, discuss/practice/explain.

Activity: take pieces of facilitator's guide cut out and reconstruct or something like that to refamiliarize with the whole of the thing we all went through last time.  Maybe just the facilitator instructions or the facilitator instructions on green paper and the participant workbook on yellow and we try to put the puzzle back together.

Activity: for some elements of the workshop talk about goals and then work in groups to generate techniques.

Activity: short catalog of tricks of the trade \- video? \- and then reactions.

Activity: role play \- use pitch and catch format and each person has a few minutes to get the other folks to do something.

## Elements of Workshop

### Ice Breaking

Purpose/goal is to transform attendees into participants. Get people to smile \- people who smile and laugh together are going to interact better. It helps to generate things we need: interactive comfort \- readiness to listen to one another, exchange ideas, learn together; emotional comfort \- people bring varied collections of shyness and pushiness, anxiety, axes that want grinding, insecurities, biases, expectations, etc. Our goal is to diffuse the effect of at least some of these at the outset.
### Who's in the Room?

We want participants to be "all in" but for this to happen they need to know who they are interacting with. Sometimes you give a workshop to a group that already knows one another and so the job is for facilitators and participants to get to know one another. But in our workshop the big hurdle will be high powered folks who want to know who the other high powered folks are.
### What's Going On Here?

What is and what is not going to happen. What common starting point are we asking folks to agree to.
### Divergent and Convergent Thinking

Basic trajectory of design thinking is to alternate between divergent thinking (expanding the space of ideas under consideration) and convergent thinking (filtering, prioritizing, selecting, implementing ideas).

Convergent thinking is the stuff of science and rational deductive analysis. Consider the problem of hunting for a lion in the desert.  First we draw a line around the whole desert. Then we ascertain whether the lion is in the west or east half of the desert.  And then the north or the south half of the remaining area. And so on until we have zeroed in on the lion.

![][image1]

This is the method of rational problem solving: define a solution space (the lion must be somewhere within these boundaries) and then continue to narrow the space until there is only one solution left. This is convergent thinking. Convergent thinking is based on algorithms that get closer and closer to the solution with each iteration.

Another geometric representation of convergent thinking is the elimination bracket we often see in sports tournaments: the 64 teams at the start represent the possible best ideas; they play games and one by one eliminate teams until only the champion remains.

Divergent thinking presumes that best solution is "out there" in the minds and experiences of people \- people who live a problem that we are trying to solve and people who are trying to solve the problem \- but that all sorts of ordinary human and social tendencies make those solutions hard to find.  

Conventional problem solving has a name for the initial boundary around the solution space \- we call it the "feasible set," the things it makes sense to spend time considering. Anything not in the feasible set cannot be a solution so why waste time thinking about it.  But in practice, "feasible" translates to conventional, acceptable, easy, obvious, what-we-do, what-the-client-wants, things-we-have-done-before.  And feasible things are the ones vocalized by the loudest, gregarious-est, quickest to the microphone, male-est, senior-est, etc. members of the team.

Divergent thinking is not a strictly individual activity. When we do it with others, each person's out-of-the-box idea can redefine the space of possibilities for others.

### Brainstorming

Don't think of brainstorming as a particular technique (e.g., just yell out ideas and we'll record them on sticky notes or a white board). It is a goal rather than a method.  The brain that is connected to our mouth or our hand is constrained by bias. We think of the most obvious things first, the things which sound like what we've most recently encountered, the things that we think will be most acceptable to the people around us, the things we think will make us look smart or cool or morally good.  In a brainstorming activity we encourage volume because we need to get all the ideas that are prioritized by these motivations out of the way so we can get to the new and potentially insightful stuff. We literally are trying to elicit the unheard of from ourselves.

Exercise: think of alternative ways to "brainstorm" that address issues such as 

* most first ideas are trite  
* some people need to think quietly in order to generate ideas  
* some people are better in give and take conversation than just writing stuff down  
* some people are super shy about sharing their ideas

### Empathy

You'll hear the word "empathy" a lot in connection with user-centered design and human-centered design.  It's related to the mantra "YOU ARE NOT THE USER" which exhorts designers not to fall into the trap of designing for themselves.  In our context we would usually mean encouraging experts to see beyond their own bailiwick and "designing" for the needs of the whole stakeholder ecosystem under consideration.

Our pursuit of empathy often has us doing "user research" and representing what we find in terms of "empathy maps" that capture what users say, think, do, feel, etc. about a product/problem/task that we are designing for.  In our context we will sometimes speak of "organizational empathy maps" or "stakeholder empathy maps." We use these to develop a shared sense of who all the stakeholders are (since a policy or institutional design process usually doesn't have a single, simple user) and what makes each one "tick" which we break down as 

* what's success and failure for them?  
* what professional culture dominates their mindset?  
* who are their peers, competitors, etc.?  
* what do they measure?  
* what incentivises them?   
* what's their "kryptonite"?

### Insights

### Prototyping

An important insight from design is the value of getting ideas out of our heads and into concrete, palpable form so that people can interact with them, provide experience-based feedback.  The alternative is  intellectual analysis in which we simulate the world and other people in our heads and assess how our ideas would play out. But why waste all that cognitive processing power simulating the world and other people when they are both right here?  

Sometimes we prototype for ourselves \- a proof of concept to show that something is realizable at all in the real world. Sometimes we prototype to communicate essential properties of an idea to others; we hope for a reaction such as "now I see what you were getting at\!" or "oh, so it works like this" to which you say "oh, wow, I hadn't seen that at all\!"  Sometimes we prototype as an experiment to reveal answers to empirical questions like which material works better or how long would it take our organization to carry out some process.  And sometimes we prototype as a way of getting information from users that we could never elicit just by asking (e.g., which kinds of buttons would be easier to use?").

## Concrete Stuff

Why turn taking, timing, writing stuff down.  These are various techniques for obviating human tendencies and obstacles generated by individual differences (some of us are too loud or too quiet, gregarious, not very good at listening, dominated by ego bias or confirmation bias or selection bias or recency bias, some are verbose and take up all the time if not limited, some won't speak up unless a space is created, some don't think before they speak or don't do a good job of speaking their thoughts, etc. etc. etc.).
